title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds to a Public Discourse article by eight SBC seminary professors and executives opposing the 2021 Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion. He defends the resolution against charges that it ignores ectopic pregnancies, demands overnight change, lacks means, and harbors misogyny, and he indicts pro-life leaders for treating SCOTUS as a quasi-divine authority.
audiences: Southern Baptist pastors and seminarians weighing the resolution
Pro-life leaders and ERLC supporters skeptical of abolitionism
Abolitionist activists and equal protection advocates
Christians wrestling with civil disobedience and judicial supremacy
Pro-life voters confused about ectopic pregnancy treatment

questions.question: Why does the resolution not explicitly address ectopic pregnancies, and is that an oversight?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters confused about ectopic pregnancy treatment
questions.answer: It is not an oversight. The authors deliberately omitted ectopic pregnancies because the standard treatment is not properly classified as abortion. Removing a baby who has already died is not abortion, and the preterm delivery of a baby with no chance of survival is morally distinct from the intentional destruction of the child by D&E or D&C. Conflating the two slanders grieving parents and gives moral cover to actual abortion.
questions.quote: The authors did not explicitly address ectopic pregnancies because the treatment of ectopic pregnancies is not properly defined as abortion.

questions.question: What amendment did abolitionists prepare in case the convention insisted on addressing ectopic pregnancy?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastors and seminarians weighing the resolution
questions.answer: Lacey Adamson, an abolitionist nurse and messenger from Ekklesia Muskogee, was prepared to offer an amendment between articles 13 and 14 framing ectopic pregnancy as a tragedy requiring medical triage in which doctors treat both mother and baby as human beings and exhaust all options under the Hippocratic Oath. The article holds this up as the proper template for any future SBC language on the topic, rather than the professors' approach of equating treatment with abortion.
questions.quote: (14) RESOLVED, that we recognize that ectopic pregnancies are a tragedy during which medical triage is necessary, with doctors never ceasing to treat both mother and baby as human beings, and upholding the Hippocratic Oath by exhausting all possible options to save as many human lives as they can.

questions.question: Does abolitionism actually expect abortion to end overnight, as the SBC professors' Normandy analogy implies?
questions.audience: Pro-life leaders and ERLC supporters skeptical of abolitionism
questions.answer: No. Silberman calls the Normandy analogy a sign of either ignorance or bad faith. Abolitionists, like their 19th century predecessors, expect a long, costly fight. The dispute with incrementalism is not about timeline but about what we demand: immediate, principled abolition versus compromise legislation that ratifies the legality of killing some preborn children.
questions.quote: abolitionism is not overnight-ism. Abolitionists are not under the impression that the culture and politics will drastically change overnight or that the object of our protest will be abolished overnight.

questions.question: How does William Lloyd Garrison's framing illuminate the abolitionist demand?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists and equal protection advocates
questions.answer: Garrison conceded that even the most urgent demand for immediate abolition would in practice unfold gradually, but insisted the demand itself must remain uncompromising. The article uses this to show that the abolition-vs-incrementalism debate is about the content of the demand, not the duration of the struggle. Abolitionists set a high principled bar and let the culture move toward them.
questions.quote: Urge immediate abolition as earnestly as we may, it will alas! be gradual abolition in the end. We have never said that slavery would be overthrown by a single blow; that it ought to be we shall always contend.

questions.question: Why does the article say incremental pro-life bills cannot be grounded in Scripture or the Constitution?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with civil disobedience and judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Because incremental laws like 20-week bans or heartbeat bills implicitly concede that abortion before some arbitrary line is permissible. That concession violates both the Sixth Commandment and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection clause. By contrast, the abolitionist demand for total abolition can be defended directly from 'You shall not murder' and from equal protection under the law.
questions.quote: An incrementalist's demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God's Word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

questions.question: How does the article turn Proverbs 24:11, the professors' lone proof text, against them?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastors and seminarians weighing the resolution
questions.answer: Silberman points out that the verse contains no exception for state-sanctioned killing. The pro-life claim that 'all can't be saved right now' presupposes unconditional submission to Roe and Casey, treating SCOTUS opinions as binding even when they authorize murder. He argues the verse actually demands the opposite of incremental compromise: it demands rescue of those being led to slaughter, full stop.
questions.quote: Notice that Proverbs 24:11 does not say, "Deliver those who are being taken away to death, unless the supreme court authorizes their murder."

questions.question: What does the article identify as idolatry in mainstream pro-life leadership?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with civil disobedience and judicial supremacy
questions.answer: It points to statements from figures like Joe Pojman of Texas Alliance for Life and Scott Klusendorf of Life Training Institute that frame SCOTUS authority as a law of nature or as 'on high.' Silberman calls this an idolatrous reduction of allegiance to God into a temporary alliance whenever God's Word and the Court happen to agree. Civil authorities are due only conditional submission, conditioned on their own submission to Christ.
questions.quote: Equating the authority of the supreme court to a law of nature or referring to the justices with phrases related to divinity are a form of idolatry.

questions.question: What concrete means does the resolution propose for actually abolishing abortion?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists and equal protection advocates
questions.answer: Article 9 of the resolution names interposition by governing authorities at every level: intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous SCOTUS decisions and establishing equal protection for all, born and preborn. The article cites Daniel 3, 1 Kings 12, 2 Kings 11, and Jeremiah 26, 36, 37, and 39 as biblical warrant. Silberman dismisses the professors' suggestion that the means require armed resistance or civil war as scare tactics; abolitionism has always been a peaceful movement.
questions.quote: (9) WHEREAS, governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish equal protection under the law for all, born and preborn, by intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous decisions when other authorities, such as the Supreme Court, condone such injustices as the legal taking of innocent life

questions.question: Why does the article cite the SBC's resolution on the Uyghur genocide as a parallel?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastors and seminarians weighing the resolution
questions.answer: Resolution #8 demanded that China cease its Uyghur genocide 'immediately,' with no incremental qualifier. Silberman argues that any messenger who proposed weakening that demand to merely gradual cessation would have been instantly recognized as failing the oppressed. The same standard exposes the absurdity of demanding only gradual cessation of abortion.
questions.quote: Had a messenger suggested weakening this language to demand only the gradual ceasing of the genocide, it would have been immediately recognized as a failure to be consistently Christian, and a failure to make a strong stand for the oppressed Uyghur people.

questions.question: How does the article respond to the charge that abolitionists 'fail to act in the day of distress'?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists and equal protection advocates
questions.answer: Silberman calls the accusation evidence of ignorance. Abolitionist rallies at state capitols dwarf incremental pro-life rallies, and abolitionists outnumber pro-lifers at most abortion mills. The movement deliberately reserves the term 'abolitionist' for people who actually act; mere holders of a pro-life opinion are not counted.
questions.quote: There is not a more active group of people anywhere for any cause than the abolitionists.

questions.question: Is the resolution misogynistic for hinting at prosecution of post-abortive women?
questions.audience: Pro-life leaders and ERLC supporters skeptical of abolitionism
questions.answer: Silberman calls this an outright lie. The resolution simply demands equal protection under the law for preborn children, which logically entails the possible prosecution of any culpable person, man or woman, who knowingly participates in the murder. He says conflating equality for the preborn with misogyny is the rhetorical move of pro-aborts, and he calls on the SBC professors to issue a correction and apology.
questions.quote: The willful conflation of the abolitionist desire for equality for preborn children with misogyny is the tactic of pro-aborts. The SBC professors lied, sinning against God and the pastors who authored the resolution.

questions.question: What is Silberman's overall verdict on the SBC professors' Public Discourse article?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastors and seminarians weighing the resolution
questions.answer: Despite the credentials of its eight signatories, the article was almost entirely without scriptural reference, and its one biblical argument from Proverbs 24:11 was worse than no argument at all. Silberman concludes the piece is at times ignorant and at times dishonest, and that its authors should unpublish it.
questions.quote: At times ignorant, and at times dishonest, this woefully poor attempt at refuting the Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion should be unpublished by the authors.

related.title: Refuting Dana Hall McCain's Nonsensical Anti-Abolitionist Slander
related.reason: Earlier installment in the same FTS series responding to pro-life critics of the SBC abolition resolution, and quoted directly in this article.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Companion installment in the SBC response series, also engaging Scott Klusendorf, whose SCOTUS-as-divine framing is central to this piece.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: Reports the passage of the very resolution this article is defending, and is hyperlinked in the opening paragraph.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Linked from this article as point #3 evidence that incremental bills can move the needle in the wrong direction.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Develops at length the same critique of pro-life compromise and SCOTUS deference that this article applies to the SBC professors.

related.title: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
related.reason: Provides the broader SBC context in which Burk, Walker, and the other professors mounted their opposition.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Concise framing of the core demand-vs-timeline distinction that this article makes against the Normandy analogy.
